title: E-commerce REST API
description: Full-stack e-commerce demo built to exercise product flows, session auth, documented APIs, and versioned VPS deployment under real constraints.

introduction.title: E-commerce REST API
description: This project was built to make a portfolio e-commerce demo feel closer to a real engineering system than a static storefront: catalog browsing, session-based authentication, cart and checkout flows, API documentation, and deployment all had to work together.

The core constraint was publishing frontend and backend under one public domain while keeping backend traffic proxied through `/api`, preserving a clean user experience and a safer operational boundary.

react.description: React drives the catalog, cart, auth, and checkout screens with reusable UI patterns that keep user flows easy to extend.

vite.description: Vite keeps frontend iteration fast while testing product-flow changes across catalog, cart, and authentication screens.

react-router.description: React Router separates public, auth, cart, checkout, and documentation views while keeping navigation predictable in the SPA.

javascript.description: JavaScript keeps the demo stack consistent across frontend and Express backend, reducing context switching while building full-stack flows.

mui.description: MUI provides accessible components for forms, tables, dialogs, and product surfaces, helping the demo feel inspectable rather than throwaway.

tailwindcss.description: Tailwind CSS is used sparingly for layout refinements where quick utility styling was more efficient than custom component variants.

nodejs.description: Node.js runs the API layer and operational scripts, tying backend behavior, deployment checks, and demo reset flows into one runtime.

express.description: Express structures REST endpoints for products, carts, auth, checkout, and operational health with clear middleware boundaries.

passport.description: Passport keeps local and optional Google OAuth strategies modular, so authentication can evolve without rewriting protected routes.

express-session.description: express-session persists authenticated user state for protected cart/order flows, matching the demo goal of realistic session behavior.

postgresql.description: PostgreSQL models users, products, carts, and orders relationally, making data constraints explicit and easier to inspect.

caddy.description: Caddy publishes frontend and API through one HTTPS domain while keeping backend services reachable only through the intended proxy path.

github.description: GitHub tracks code, deploy workflow changes, and release history across frontend and backend parts of the demo.

github-actions.description: GitHub Actions automates build, deploy, and controlled activation steps so VPS releases are repeatable instead of manual.

swagger-openapi.description: Swagger/OpenAPI makes endpoints, payloads, and auth behavior visible to reviewers without requiring source-code reading first.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports refactoring and navigation across the full-stack demo, especially shared naming across routes, API handlers, and docs.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT supported deployment scripting and documentation passes, with manual validation of operational and architecture choices.

difficulties_faced: Designing robust session-based authentication (local login + optional Google OAuth) while keeping the user flow consistent.
Handling frontend/API routing through a single domain proxy without exposing backend services directly.
Building a reliable VPS deployment pipeline with versioned releases and controlled activation.
Managing demo database reset on backend deploy without compromising runtime stability and functional checks.
Documenting APIs and payloads clearly to keep the project immediately verifiable for recruiters and developers.
lessons_learned: A demo project becomes more credible when it includes operational concerns such as deploy, reset flows, and API documentation.
Session-based authentication affects both backend design and frontend UX, so it needs to be treated as a full flow rather than a single endpoint.
Keeping frontend and backend under one domain simplifies the public experience, but requires disciplined proxy and routing boundaries.
Clear Swagger/OpenAPI documentation turns backend behavior into something inspectable instead of hidden implementation detail.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Ship the monorepo foundation with React/Vite frontend, Express backend, and initial PostgreSQL schema.
Implement core product flows: catalog, cart, checkout, local authentication, and protected order behavior.
Publish through VPS/Caddy with backend access constrained to the `/api` proxy path.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Harden authentication and session flows, including optional Google OAuth and clearer protected-route behavior.
Improve API validation, error responses, and Swagger coverage so payload behavior is easier to verify.
Add operational visibility through health checks, logs, and deploy troubleshooting notes.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Add end-to-end tests for critical flows such as auth, cart, checkout, and demo reset behavior.
Strengthen contributor documentation around local setup, API contracts, and deployment operations.
Introduce baseline API performance metrics to make response-time regressions visible.
